name: North Korea
tagline: An ancient Korean heritage severed by colonization and Cold War division, forged into a self-isolating state under three generations of a single ruling family.
summary: North Korea shares the deep history of the Korean peninsula, from the legendary kingdom of Gojoseon and the Three Kingdoms to the long Goryeo and Joseon dynasties, a past common to both Koreas. After thirty-five years of Japanese colonial rule, Japan's defeat in 1945 left the peninsula divided along the 38th parallel into Soviet and American occupation zones. In the north the Democratic People's Republic of Korea was proclaimed on 9 September 1948 under Kim Il Sung, and in 1950 the Korean War broke out, ending in 1953 in an armistice rather than a peace treaty. The state Kim built around the ideology of Juche and a command economy passed dynastically to his son Kim Jong Il and grandson Kim Jong Un, endured a devastating famine in the 1990s, and developed nuclear weapons while remaining one of the world's most closed countries into the present.

founding.label: Founding of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea
founding.detail: The Democratic People's Republic of Korea was formally proclaimed in the northern half of the Korean peninsula on 9 September 1948, with Kim Il Sung as its first premier, weeks after the Republic of Korea was established in the south. The state arose from the division of Korea after the defeat of Japan in 1945, when the peninsula was split along the 38th parallel into a Soviet occupation zone in the north and an American zone in the south. The Korean nation itself is far older: tradition traces it to the legendary kingdom of Gojoseon, and statehood took historical form in the Three Kingdoms of Goguryeo, Baekje and Silla and the later Goryeo and Joseon dynasties, a shared heritage that belongs to both Koreas. The DPRK's founding institutionalized the Cold War partition into two rival Korean governments, each claiming to represent the whole peninsula.

quickFacts.label: State founded
quickFacts.value: 9 September 1948, in the northern occupation zone, under Kim Il Sung

quickFacts.label: Shared heritage
quickFacts.value: Gojoseon, the Three Kingdoms, Goryeo and Joseon, common to both Koreas

quickFacts.label: National division
quickFacts.value: Split along the 38th parallel in 1945; two Korean states from 1948

quickFacts.label: Korean War
quickFacts.value: 1950 to 1953, ended by armistice, not a peace treaty

quickFacts.label: Guiding ideology
quickFacts.value: Juche, the doctrine of self-reliance associated with Kim Il Sung

quickFacts.label: Ruling succession
quickFacts.value: Kim Il Sung to Kim Jong Il (1994) to Kim Jong Un (2011)

eras.title: Shared Korean Heritage
eras.standfirst: Long before partition, the whole peninsula shared a single history: the legendary kingdom of Gojoseon, the Three Kingdoms, and the long dynastic ages of Goryeo and Joseon, the common inheritance of both Koreas.
body: Korean tradition traces the nation's origin to Gojoseon, a kingdom said in the 13th-century Samguk yusa to have been founded in 2333 BCE by the mythical Dangun. While the founding legend is myth, Gojoseon was a real Bronze and Iron Age polity in the northern peninsula and parts of Manchuria, much of it within the territory that would later become North Korea. It fell to the Han Chinese empire in 108 BCE, after which the peninsula fragmented into smaller statelets that gradually coalesced into larger kingdoms.

From this fragmentation rose the Three Kingdoms of Goguryeo, Baekje and Silla. Goguryeo, the largest and most militarized, dominated the north and much of Manchuria; Baekje was a maritime power in the southwest; and Silla rose later in the southeast. Buddhism, introduced in the 4th century, became a unifying religious and artistic force. Allied with Tang China, Silla conquered Baekje in 660 and Goguryeo in 668 and then expelled the Tang, bringing the Korean people under a single indigenous rule for the first time in their recorded history.

Unified Silla gave way in 918 to the Goryeo dynasty, from which the Western name Korea derives, a Buddhist civilization famous for celadon ceramics and the Tripitaka Koreana woodblocks. In 1392 the general Yi Seong-gye founded the Joseon dynasty, which adopted Neo-Confucianism, ruled for more than five centuries from Hanyang (Seoul), and under King Sejong created the hangul alphabet. This long shared history belongs equally to North and South Korea; the two states diverged only with the colonial and Cold War upheavals of the 20th century.

events.title: Legendary founding of Gojoseon
events.summary: According to the Samguk yusa, the mythical Dangun establishes Gojoseon, traditionally regarded as the first Korean kingdom and a shared origin point for both Koreas.

events.title: Silla unifies the peninsula
events.summary: Allied with Tang China, Silla conquers Baekje in 660 and Goguryeo in 668, then expels the Tang, achieving the first unification of Korea under native rule.

events.title: Goryeo dynasty founded
events.summary: Wang Geon establishes the Goryeo dynasty, which reunifies the peninsula and gives rise to the Western name Korea.

events.title: Joseon dynasty founded
events.summary: Yi Seong-gye founds the long-lived Joseon dynasty, which adopts Neo-Confucianism and under King Sejong creates the hangul alphabet.

pullquote.text: The unification of the peninsula under Silla in 668 brought the Korean people under a single indigenous government for the first time in their recorded history.
pullquote.attribution: Korean history, on the early peninsula

eras.title: Colonial Rule and Division
eras.standfirst: Japan annexed Korea in 1910 and ruled it for thirty-five years; Japan's defeat in 1945 brought liberation alongside a division of the peninsula that hardened into two rival states by 1948.
body: Japan annexed Korea in 1910, ending the Joseon dynasty and beginning thirty-five years of colonial rule over the whole peninsula. The colonial government ruled through military and police power, censored the press, reorganized the economy to serve Japanese interests, and in its later years intensified forced assimilation, suppressing the Korean language and mobilizing Koreans for forced labor and wartime service. Resistance was constant, most famously in the March First Movement of 1919, and an independence struggle continued in exile and at home throughout the colonial period.

Among the Korean exiles who fought Japanese rule was Kim Il Sung, who led a Korean contingent in guerrilla and Soviet army service in Manchuria and the Russian Far East. When Japan surrendered on 15 August 1945, ending the Second World War, the peninsula was liberated, but liberation came hand in hand with partition. To accept the Japanese surrender, the United States and the Soviet Union agreed to divide Korea along the 38th parallel, the Soviets occupying the north and the Americans the south, a line chosen hastily by two American officers with little regard for Korean realities.

Efforts to reunify the two occupation zones into a single Korean government failed as Cold War rivalries deepened. In the north, Soviet authorities backed Kim Il Sung and a communist administration; in the south, a separate government took shape under American auspices. By 1948 two antagonistic states had emerged, each claiming to be the legitimate government of all Korea. The Republic of Korea was proclaimed in the south on 15 August 1948, and the Democratic People's Republic of Korea was founded in the north on 9 September 1948, completing the partition of the peninsula.

events.title: Japanese annexation of Korea
events.summary: Japan formally annexes Korea, ending the Joseon dynasty and beginning thirty-five years of colonial rule over the entire peninsula.

events.title: Liberation and division at the 38th parallel
events.summary: Japan's surrender frees Korea but the peninsula is split along the 38th parallel into Soviet and American occupation zones, setting the stage for two rival states.

events.title: Founding of the DPRK
events.summary: The Democratic People's Republic of Korea is proclaimed under Kim Il Sung, weeks after the Republic of Korea is established in the south, institutionalizing the division.

figures.name: Kim Il Sung
figures.blurb: An anti-Japanese guerrilla who fought in Manchuria and served with Soviet forces, Kim Il Sung was installed with Soviet backing and became the first premier of the DPRK in 1948. He ruled until his death in 1994, building an extensive cult of personality as the Great Leader.

pullquote.text: After Japan's defeat in 1945 the peninsula was divided along the 38th parallel, crystallizing the division between North and South that exists today.
pullquote.attribution: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

eras.title: The Korean War
eras.standfirst: The first major armed conflict of the Cold War devastated the peninsula and ended not in victory for either side but in an armistice that froze the division still in place today.
body: On 25 June 1950 North Korean forces crossed the 38th parallel into the south, opening the Korean War, the first major armed conflict of the Cold War. The North's army advanced rapidly and nearly overran the entire peninsula before the United States led a United Nations-authorized coalition to intervene. UN forces pushed the North's army back above the 38th parallel and deep into the north, until the People's Republic of China entered the war in late 1950 and drove the front back toward the original dividing line.

By late 1951 the opposing forces were deadlocked near the 38th parallel, and the conflict settled into a grinding stalemate that one Office of the Historian account compared to the trench warfare of the First World War. Truce talks at Panmunjom dragged on for two years, snagged in particular on the question of whether prisoners of war should be forcibly repatriated. Throughout the negotiations the fighting continued, and bombing and ground combat inflicted enormous destruction across both Koreas, killing several million people, a large proportion of them civilians.

On 27 July 1953 the two sides signed an armistice, halting the fighting near the line where it had begun and establishing a heavily fortified Demilitarized Zone along the front. No peace treaty followed, so the two Koreas remain technically at war to this day. The war entrenched the partition, devastated North Korea's cities and infrastructure, and shaped the country's enduring posture of militarized confrontation and reliance on its allies in Beijing and Moscow.

events.title: North Korea invades the South
events.summary: North Korean forces cross the 38th parallel, opening the Korean War; the North nearly overruns the peninsula before a US-led UN coalition intervenes.

events.title: China enters the war
events.summary: After UN forces push deep into the north, the People's Republic of China intervenes and drives the front back toward the 38th parallel, producing a stalemate.

events.title: Korean War armistice
events.summary: An armistice halts the fighting and creates the Demilitarized Zone; no peace treaty is signed, freezing the division of the peninsula to the present day.

pullquote.text: Only in 1953 did the two sides reach an uneasy truce, thus crystallizing the division between North and South that exists today.
pullquote.attribution: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

eras.title: The Juche State
eras.standfirst: From the ruins of war Kim Il Sung built a centralized command economy and a cult of personality around the doctrine of Juche, ruling for four decades and arranging a dynastic succession to his son.
body: After the armistice Kim Il Sung consolidated absolute power, sidelining and purging rivals within the Korean Workers' Party and centralizing authority around himself. He promoted a philosophy of Juche, or self-reliance, first invoked in the mid-1950s and elaborated into the guiding ideology of the state, resting on principles of political independence, economic self-sufficiency and military self-defense. The state-run command economy, rebuilt with Soviet and Chinese aid, grew rapidly through the 1950s and 1960s as the country industrialized, before stagnating in later decades.

A new Socialist Constitution adopted in 1972 created the office of president for Kim Il Sung, named Juche the guiding idea of the Workers' Party, and confirmed Pyongyang as the capital. An elaborate cult of personality cast Kim as the Great Leader at the center of national life, and the regime cultivated a pervasive ideology celebrating the leader, the party and the nation. Maneuvering between the Soviet Union and China as their dispute deepened, North Korea preserved its autonomy while leaning on both for trade, technology and security guarantees.

From the 1970s Kim Il Sung groomed his son Kim Jong Il as successor, formally designating him in 1980 and gradually transferring party and military authority to him, an unprecedented hereditary succession in a communist state. When Kim Il Sung died of a heart attack in July 1994, he had ruled for nearly five decades. He was posthumously declared Eternal President, and power passed to Kim Jong Il, who would lead the country through the gravest crisis in its history.

events.title: Socialist Constitution adopted
events.summary: A new constitution creates the presidency for Kim Il Sung, enshrines Juche as the Workers' Party's guiding idea, and confirms Pyongyang as the capital.

events.title: Kim Jong Il designated successor
events.summary: Kim Il Sung formally designates his son Kim Jong Il as his successor, beginning an unprecedented hereditary transfer of power in a communist state.

events.title: North and South Korea join the UN
events.summary: Both Korean states are admitted to the United Nations with separate seats, an acknowledgment of the durability of the two-state division.

events.title: Death of Kim Il Sung
events.summary: Kim Il Sung dies after ruling for nearly five decades; he is declared Eternal President and power passes to his son Kim Jong Il.

figures.name: Kim Il Sung
figures.blurb: North Korea's founder ruled from 1948 until his death in 1994, building a command economy and the Juche ideology and an extensive cult of personality. He launched the 1950 invasion of the South and arranged a hereditary succession to his son.

pullquote.text: Kim introduced a philosophy of Juche, or self-reliance, under which North Korea tried to develop its economy with little or no help from foreign countries.
pullquote.attribution: Encyclopaedia Britannica, on Kim Il Sung

eras.title: Famine and the Second Succession
eras.standfirst: Under Kim Jong Il the country suffered a catastrophic famine in the 1990s, pursued nuclear weapons through years of crisis and negotiation, and prepared a third generation of dynastic rule.
body: Kim Jong Il, often titled the Dear Leader, became de facto ruler on his father's death in 1994 and formally took the country's highest posts by 1998. His rule opened amid catastrophe. The collapse of the Soviet Union ended vital subsidies and trade, China reduced its support, and severe flooding in the mid-1990s destroyed much of the harvest, causing the food rationing system to fail. The resulting famine, which the regime called the Arduous March, killed an estimated several hundred thousand to perhaps more than a million people between roughly 1994 and 1999, one of the defining traumas of the country's modern history.

Amid the crisis North Korea advanced a long-running nuclear weapons program that became the central issue in its relations with the outside world. After withdrawing from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty in 2003 and expelling international inspectors, North Korea conducted its first underground nuclear test on 9 October 2006. Cycles of negotiation followed, including the Six-Party Talks involving China, the United States, Russia, Japan and the two Koreas, which produced agreements on denuclearization steps that repeatedly broke down. A landmark inter-Korean summit between Kim Jong Il and South Korea's Kim Dae-jung had been held in Pyongyang in 2000.

Despite famine, sanctions and economic hardship, Kim Jong Il maintained the family's grip on power and the priority of the military under a Songun, or military-first, policy. In his final years, with his health failing, he moved to secure a third hereditary succession, promoting his youngest son Kim Jong Un and granting him senior party and military rank. When Kim Jong Il died in December 2011, Kim Jong Un, then in his late twenties, inherited leadership of the state and its growing nuclear arsenal.

events.title: The Arduous March famine
events.summary: A famine driven by the loss of Soviet support, reduced Chinese aid and flooding kills an estimated several hundred thousand or more North Koreans, a defining national trauma.

events.title: First inter-Korean summit
events.summary: Kim Jong Il hosts South Korean president Kim Dae-jung in Pyongyang in the first summit between the two Koreas, briefly easing tensions.

events.title: First nuclear test
events.summary: North Korea conducts its first underground nuclear test, having withdrawn from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty in 2003 and cycled through failed negotiations.

events.title: Death of Kim Jong Il
events.summary: Kim Jong Il dies and is succeeded by his youngest son Kim Jong Un, completing a third generation of hereditary rule.

figures.name: Kim Jong Il
figures.blurb: Designated his father's successor in 1980, Kim Jong Il became de facto leader in 1994 and ruled through the 1990s famine and the development of nuclear weapons under a military-first policy. He died in December 2011 after arranging the succession of his son.

pullquote.text: North Korea's state-run economy grew rapidly in the 1950s and '60s but eventually stagnated, with shortages of food occurring by the early '90s.

eras.title: The Kim Jong Un Era
eras.standfirst: The third Kim consolidated power, expanded the nuclear and missile arsenal, conducted unprecedented summit diplomacy that ultimately stalled, and redefined the state's relationship with the South.
body: Kim Jong Un took power in 2011 and moved to consolidate his authority, including the 2013 execution of his uncle and one-time mentor Jang Song-thaek. He accelerated the nuclear and missile programs his father had pursued, conducting further underground nuclear tests, including the country's largest in 2017, and a rapid series of ballistic missile launches. North Korea declared itself a nuclear weapons state and enshrined that status in law, while the United Nations Security Council imposed successive rounds of sanctions in response to the tests.

In 2018 and 2019 Kim Jong Un undertook a striking burst of diplomacy. He met South Korean president Moon Jae-in at the inter-Korean border and became the first North Korean leader to meet a sitting U.S. president, holding a summit with Donald Trump in Singapore in June 2018 that produced a joint commitment to work toward denuclearization. A second summit in Hanoi in February 2019 collapsed without agreement over sanctions relief, and a brief meeting at the Demilitarized Zone later that year did not revive the process. Negotiations stalled, and North Korea resumed and intensified weapons testing.

Through the 2020s North Korea further expanded its arsenal, testing intercontinental ballistic missiles and codifying an assertive nuclear doctrine, while strengthening ties with Russia. In a significant shift, in late 2023 and early 2024 Kim Jong Un abandoned the long-standing goal of peaceful reunification, declaring South Korea a hostile, separate state and the country's principal enemy and calling for the constitution to be revised accordingly. Into 2026 North Korea remains one of the world's most closed and tightly controlled states, ruled by the third generation of the Kim family and committed to its nuclear program.

events.title: Largest nuclear test and ICBM launches
events.summary: North Korea conducts its most powerful nuclear test and tests intercontinental ballistic missiles, prompting expanded UN Security Council sanctions.

events.title: Singapore summit with the United States
events.summary: Kim Jong Un becomes the first North Korean leader to meet a sitting U.S. president, signing a joint statement with Donald Trump on working toward denuclearization.

events.title: Hanoi summit collapses
events.summary: A second Trump-Kim summit in Hanoi ends without agreement over sanctions and denuclearization, after which diplomacy stalls and weapons testing resumes.

events.title: South Korea declared principal enemy
events.summary: Kim Jong Un renounces the goal of peaceful reunification, declaring South Korea a hostile separate state and the North's principal enemy and calling for constitutional revision.

figures.name: Kim Jong Un
figures.blurb: The grandson of Kim Il Sung, Kim Jong Un took power in 2011 and consolidated control while expanding the nuclear and missile arsenal. He held unprecedented summits with U.S. and South Korean leaders in 2018-2019 and later declared South Korea the North's principal enemy.

pullquote.text: Kim Jong Un became the first North Korean leader to meet a sitting U.S. president, holding a summit with Donald Trump in Singapore in June 2018.
pullquote.attribution: Arms Control Association, chronology of U.S.-North Korean diplomacy

figures.name: Kim Il Sung
figures.role: Founder and first leader of North Korea
figures.blurb: An anti-Japanese guerrilla installed with Soviet backing, he became premier of the DPRK in 1948, launched the 1950 invasion of the South, and ruled until 1994, founding the Juche ideology and a hereditary dynasty.

figures.name: Kim Jong Il
figures.blurb: Succeeding his father in 1994, he led North Korea through the 1990s famine and the development of nuclear weapons before passing power to his son in 2011.

figures.name: Kim Jong Un
figures.blurb: Taking power in 2011, he expanded the nuclear arsenal, held landmark summits with U.S. and South Korean leaders, and later declared South Korea a hostile separate state.
